problem: Suppose that all four of the numbers \[3 - 2\sqrt{2}, \; -3-2\sqrt{2}, \; 1+\sqrt{7}, \; 1-\sqrt{7}\]are roots of the same nonzero polynomial with rational coefficients. What is the smallest possible degree of the polynomial?

classified_questions.question: What is the concept of radical conjugates? How do we determine the radical conjugate of a given root?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: Why must the polynomial have at least six roots in order to have all four given roots as its roots?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: How do we generate a polynomial with given roots? What is the connection between the roots and the factors of the polynomial?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How do we determine the degree of a polynomial? Can a polynomial have more than one degree?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How might we approach this problem if we didn't know the factorization of the polynomial? Can we find the degree of the polynomial without explicitly constructing it?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Are there any other general properties of polynomials with rational coefficients that might help us approach this problem?
classified_questions.label: r
